goal_title_suggestions: Learn to Day Trade Without Losing Real Money
Build a Paper Trading System in 90 Days
Master One Setup Before Ever Going Live

variants: Get the app. Download Webull from the App Store or Google Play.
Install Webull. Search 'Webull' in your app store and tap install.
Set up your tool. Download the free Webull app right now.
tasks.why: Webull is free and lets you practice without real money.

variants: Open the chart. Pull up SPY in Webull and look at it for 60 seconds.
Watch SPY. Find SPY in Webull and see if the line goes up or down.
Check SPY. Tap on SPY in Webull and stare at the chart for 1 minute.
tasks.why: SPY is the most-watched stock — see it once today.

variants: Watch a quick video. Search 'stocks for beginners 2 minutes' and play one.
Learn the basics. Find a 1-min YouTube clip explaining what stocks are.
Get context. Watch one short video on what buying a stock actually means.
tasks.why: One video gives you the vocabulary for everything ahead.

variants: Enable paper trading. In Webull, tap 'Paper Trading' and activate it.
Turn on fake money. Find the Paper Trading mode inside Webull and switch it on.
Activate practice mode. Open Webull settings and enable Paper Trading.
tasks.why: Paper trading lets you practice buying and selling with zero real money.

variants: Check SPY. Open SPY in Webull and note if it went up or down today.
Read the price. Look at SPY in Webull and write down today's closing price.
Watch SPY move. Pull up SPY in Webull and see what today looked like.
tasks.why: Checking the same stock daily builds your eye for what's normal.

variants: Write one observation. Jot one sentence about what SPY did today.
Note what you saw. Write down one thing you noticed on the chart.
Log it. Write 'SPY went up' or 'SPY went down' in a notes app.
tasks.why: Writing forces you to actually pay attention.

variants: Find paper trading. Tap through Webull menus until you see the Paper Trading button.
Locate your practice area. Find where Paper Trading lives in the Webull app.
Navigate Webull. Open Webull and find the Paper Trading tab without help.
tasks.why: Knowing where things are saves time when you want to practice a trade.

variants: Watch SPY's chart. Open SPY in Webull and look at the 1-day chart for 60 seconds.
Observe SPY. Spend 1 minute watching SPY's price line move in Webull.
Check SPY today. Pull up SPY and notice if it's trending up or down.
tasks.why: Daily chart-watching is the simplest habit a trader can build.

variants: Learn one word. Search 'what is a stock ticker' and read one short answer.
Define a term. Look up 'bid price' and write a one-line definition.
Learn the lingo. Find out what 'market cap' means and write it down.
tasks.why: One new word a day builds your trading vocabulary fast.

variants: Write down SPY's price. Open Webull, find SPY, and note the current price.
Record SPY today. Log SPY's price right now in a notes app.
Check SPY's number. Pull up SPY in Webull and write down what you see.
tasks.why: Tracking the price daily teaches you what 'normal' looks like.

variants: Watch a chart video. Search 'how to read a stock chart for beginners' and watch 2 min.
Learn charts. Find a short clip showing what the lines on a chart mean.
Get chart basics. Watch a 2-min video explaining candlestick or line charts.
tasks.why: Charts tell the story of a stock — one video makes them less confusing.

variants: Define one term. Write down what 'volume' means in trading.
Learn a chart word. Look up 'support level' and write one sentence about it.
Write one definition. Find out what 'resistance' means and write it down.
tasks.why: Learning one word per session compounds into real knowledge.

variants: Check SPY. Open Webull and look at SPY's 1-day chart for 1 minute.
Watch SPY move. Spend 60 seconds looking at SPY's chart in Webull.
Pull up SPY. Open Webull, find SPY, and just observe for 1 minute.
tasks.why: Daily repetition makes patterns start to feel familiar.

variants: Find AAPL. Open Webull, search AAPL, and look at its chart for 1 minute.
Explore AAPL. Pull up Apple stock in Webull and see what its chart looks like.
Add a second stock. Find AAPL in Webull and look at its price today.
tasks.why: AAPL is one of the most-traded stocks — it's worth knowing.

variants: Compare two stocks. Look at SPY and AAPL side by side and note one difference.
Spot a difference. Compare SPY's chart to AAPL's and write one observation.
Check both charts. Open SPY then AAPL in Webull and note which moved more.
tasks.why: Comparing two stocks helps you see how they can move differently.

variants: Check SPY. Open Webull and glance at SPY's price for 60 seconds.
Daily SPY check. Pull up SPY and note if it's higher or lower than yesterday.
Look at SPY. Open Webull and see what SPY is doing right now.
tasks.why: Consistency beats intensity — even 60 seconds counts.

variants: Watch a volume video. Search 'what is volume in stocks' and watch a 2-min clip.
Learn about volume. Find a short video showing what trading volume means.
Understand volume. Look up 'stock volume for beginners' and watch one short video.
tasks.why: Volume tells you how many people are trading — it's a key signal.

variants: Check SPY's volume. Open SPY in Webull and find where volume is shown.
Find the volume number. Look at SPY in Webull and write down today's volume.
Read the volume bar. Open SPY's chart and note whether volume is high or low today.
tasks.why: Seeing volume on a real chart makes the concept click.

variants: Weekly recap. Write 2 things you learned this week about trading.
Reflect on week 1. List 2 words or ideas you remember from this week.
Quick review. Write down one chart thing and one app thing you learned.
tasks.why: Pausing to recall what you learned makes it stick.

variants: Watch an intro video. Search 'day trading for beginners' and watch 2 minutes.
Get the big picture. Find a short video that explains what day traders do.
See how it works. Watch a 2-min clip showing a real day trader in action.
tasks.why: Seeing the full picture motivates you and shows where you're headed.

variants: Check SPY. Open SPY in Webull and write down today's price and volume.
Log SPY. Pull up SPY and record its price and whether volume is high or low.
SPY snapshot. Open Webull, find SPY, and note price + volume in one line.
tasks.why: Tracking two data points builds the habit of reading a chart.

variants: Open Webull. Pull up SPY and look at the 5-minute chart today.
Switch timeframes. Find SPY's 5-min chart in Webull and look at it for 60 seconds.
Try 5-min view. Change SPY's chart to 5-minute bars in Webull.
tasks.why: Day traders use the 5-min chart — getting familiar with it starts now.

variants: Learn candlesticks. Search 'what is a candlestick chart' and watch a 2-min video.
Watch a candle video. Find a short clip explaining what the colored candle bars mean.
Understand candles. Search 'red and green candles stocks' and watch one short video.
tasks.why: Candlestick charts show open, high, low, and close — day traders use them constantly.

variants: Find green and red candles. Open SPY's 5-min chart in Webull and count 3 green candles.
Spot the candles. Look at SPY's 5-min chart and point out one red candle.
Read candles. Open SPY in Webull, switch to 5-min view, and identify 2 candles.
tasks.why: Seeing candles on a real chart turns the video lesson into real knowledge.

variants: Check SPY on 5-min. Open SPY's 5-minute chart and watch it for 60 seconds.
5-min habit. Pull up SPY's 5-min chart in Webull today.
Daily 5-min check. Open Webull, switch SPY to 5-min chart, observe for 1 minute.
tasks.why: The 5-min chart is your main view as a day trader — build the habit now.

variants: Learn support. Search 'support and resistance for beginners' and watch 2 minutes.
Watch a level video. Find a short clip explaining what support means in trading.
Understand price levels. Watch a 2-min beginner video on support and resistance lines.
tasks.why: Support and resistance are the two most important price concepts in day trading.

variants: Spot a support level. Look at SPY's 5-min chart and draw a line where price bounced.
Find a level. Look at SPY's chart and write down a price it kept bouncing off of.
Mark a level. Open SPY in Webull and note a price that looks like a floor.
tasks.why: Drawing a level on a real chart makes the concept tangible.

variants: Check SPY. Open SPY's 5-min chart and write down today's opening price.
Log the open. Pull up SPY in Webull and note what price it opened at today.
Record SPY's open. Find SPY's first candle on the 5-min chart and write that price.
tasks.why: The opening price is a key reference for day traders every session.

variants: Learn about trends. Search 'what is an uptrend in stocks' and watch a 2-min clip.
Watch a trend video. Find a short video showing what uptrend and downtrend look like.
Understand direction. Watch 2 minutes of a beginner video on stock trends.
tasks.why: Trading with the trend is rule #1 for beginners — learn to see it.

variants: Name SPY's trend. Look at SPY's 5-min chart and write 'uptrend' or 'downtrend'.
Read the direction. Open SPY in Webull and decide if today was an up day or down day.
Check the trend. Look at SPY's chart and write one sentence about which way it moved.
tasks.why: Naming the trend each day builds your reading instinct.

variants: Check SPY. Open SPY's 5-min chart and spot one uptrend or downtrend.
Daily trend check. Pull up SPY and decide: is today trending up or down?
Read SPY's direction. Open Webull, find SPY's 5-min chart, and note the trend.
tasks.why: Recognizing the trend before you trade prevents costly mistakes.

variants: Learn moving averages. Search 'moving average for beginners' and watch 2 minutes.
Watch an MA video. Find a short clip explaining what a moving average line is.
Understand the MA. Watch a 2-min beginner video on the 9 EMA or 20 MA.
tasks.why: Moving averages are the most-used tool in day trading — learn the idea now.

variants: Add a moving average. Turn on the 9 EMA line on SPY's chart in Webull.
Find the MA line. Add the 20-period moving average to SPY in Webull.
See the MA. Open SPY in Webull and add any moving average line to the chart.
tasks.why: Seeing the line on your own chart beats watching it on a video.

variants: Check SPY with MA. Open SPY's 5-min chart with the moving average line visible.
MA observation. Pull up SPY, make sure the MA line is on, and look at it for 60 seconds.
Read SPY + MA. Open SPY's chart with the MA on and note: is price above or below it?
tasks.why: Price above the MA = buyers in control; below = sellers in control.

variants: Learn about entry points. Search 'when to enter a trade beginners' and watch 2 minutes.
Watch an entry video. Find a short clip explaining what a trade entry means.
Understand entries. Watch a 2-min beginner video on how traders decide when to buy.
tasks.why: Knowing when to enter is the core skill — a quick video shows the idea.

variants: Spot an entry. Look at SPY's chart and mark one spot where you'd want to buy.
Find a buy signal. Look at SPY's 5-min chart and circle one good-looking entry.
Imagine an entry. Open SPY's chart and write down at what price you'd want to buy.
tasks.why: Practicing entries on a real chart — even mentally — builds decision-making.

variants: Check SPY. Look at SPY's 5-min chart and note today's trend and MA position.
Daily read. Open SPY in Webull, check the trend, and note if price is above the MA.
SPY snapshot. Pull up SPY's 5-min chart and write: trend direction + above/below MA.
tasks.why: Two data points per day builds a real reading habit.

variants: Learn about stop losses. Search 'what is a stop loss trading' and watch 2 minutes.
Watch a stop-loss video. Find a short clip explaining why traders set stop losses.
Understand stops. Watch a 2-min beginner video on how to protect yourself in a trade.
tasks.why: A stop loss is how you limit losses — it's the most important risk tool.

variants: Write a rule. Write: 'I will not lose more than X dollars on one trade.'
Set your limit. Decide the max you'd lose on a paper trade and write it down.
Define your stop. Write one sentence: 'If I buy SPY at X, I'll sell if it drops to Y.'
tasks.why: Writing a stop-loss rule makes it real before you ever place a trade.

variants: Week 2 recap. Write 3 things you learned this week about charts or trading.
Review week 2. List candlesticks, trends, and moving averages — write one thing about each.
Reflect on progress. Write down the 2 most useful things you've learned so far.
tasks.why: Reviewing at the end of each week locks in what you've learned.

variants: Write your 3 rules. Write 3 simple rules you'll follow when paper trading.
Set your trading rules. List 3 personal rules: entry, stop loss, and time limit.
Draft your plan. Write: 1 rule for when to buy, 1 for when to sell, 1 for max loss.
tasks.why: Having 3 rules before you place a trade keeps you from winging it.

variants: Check SPY. Open SPY's 5-min chart and do your daily read: trend + MA + volume.
Daily SPY scan. Pull up SPY and write trend, MA position, and high or low volume.
Log SPY's state. Open SPY in Webull and note 3 things: price, trend, volume.
tasks.why: Consistent daily scanning becomes your pre-trade routine.

variants: Place your first paper trade. Open Webull Paper Trading, find SPY, and buy 1 share.
Make a fake trade. Go into Webull's Paper Trading mode and buy 1 share of SPY.
Try a paper buy. Open Webull Paper Trading and place a buy order for SPY.
tasks.why: Your first paper trade shows you how the order process actually works.

variants: Check your open trade. Open Webull Paper Trading and see where your SPY trade stands.
Watch your position. Look at your paper trade in Webull and see if it's up or down.
Monitor the trade. Open Webull Paper Trading and find your open SPY position.
tasks.why: Watching a live paper trade teaches you how price movement feels.

variants: Write how it felt. Write one sentence about what it was like to place a trade.
Note your reaction. Write: did placing the trade feel scary, exciting, or boring?
Log your first trade. Write the entry price and how you felt when you hit buy.
tasks.why: Trading emotions matter — noticing them early builds self-awareness.

variants: Close your paper trade. Sell your SPY position in Webull Paper Trading today.
Exit the trade. Go into Webull Paper Trading and close your open SPY position.
Sell your paper SPY. Open Webull Paper Trading and place a sell order on your SPY.
tasks.why: Learning to exit a trade is just as important as entering.

variants: Count your result. Check your paper trade result — did you make or lose fake money?
See the outcome. Open Webull Paper Trading and read your profit or loss number.
Check the P&L. Find the profit/loss number from your SPY paper trade.
tasks.why: Seeing the P&L number makes the result feel real even in paper trading.

variants: Write a trade note. Write what you bought, when, and what happened.
Log trade #1. Write: entry price, exit price, and whether you followed your rules.
Review your trade. Write one thing you'd do the same and one thing you'd change.
tasks.why: A one-trade review builds the habit of learning from every trade.

variants: Take a new paper trade. Open Webull Paper Trading and buy SPY using your 3 rules.
Place trade #2. Go into Paper Trading in Webull and buy SPY based on your written rules.
Trade with your rules. Open Webull Paper Trading, check your rules, then buy SPY.
tasks.why: Following written rules on every trade builds discipline from day one.

variants: Check SPY's trend. Before you trade more, confirm SPY is in an uptrend today.
Read the chart. Look at SPY's 5-min chart and confirm the trend direction.
Check before trading. Look at SPY's MA and trend before placing any order.
tasks.why: Checking the trend before every trade is rule #1 for beginners.

variants: Set a stop loss. In your paper trade, decide the price where you'd sell if it drops.
Plan your exit. Write at what price you'd sell SPY if the trade goes against you.
Define the stop. Write: 'I'll sell if SPY drops below X' before the trade closes.
tasks.why: Deciding your stop before the trade removes emotion from the exit.

variants: Check your paper trade. Open Webull Paper Trading and see where today's trade stands.
Monitor the position. Look at your open paper trade in Webull right now.
Check the P&L. Open Webull Paper Trading and read your current profit or loss.
tasks.why: Monitoring a live trade builds awareness of how price moves in real time.

variants: Close the trade. Sell your SPY paper trade and record the result.
Exit and log. Close your open paper trade in Webull and write the P&L.
Sell and note. Close the trade in Paper Trading and write down what happened.
tasks.why: Every closed trade is data — log it every time.

variants: Review today's trade. Write: what was the trend, did you follow your rules, and what was the result?
Trade debrief. Write 2 sentences: what went right and what you'd change.
Log it. Write entry, exit, result, and one thing you learned.
tasks.why: Short reviews after every trade are how you actually improve.

variants: Read your 3 rules out loud. Open your notes and say your trading rules to yourself.
Check your plan. Find the 3 rules you wrote and read them before doing anything.
Review your rules. Open your notes and read your entry, stop, and max-loss rules.
tasks.why: Reading your rules before trading keeps you from making impulsive decisions.

variants: Take a paper trade on AAPL. Open Webull Paper Trading and buy 1 share of AAPL.
Try a new stock. Go to Webull Paper Trading and buy AAPL using your rules.
Trade AAPL today. Open Paper Trading in Webull and place a buy on AAPL.
tasks.why: Trading a second stock shows you that your rules apply everywhere.

variants: Close and log. Sell your AAPL paper trade and write the result.
Exit and review. Close the AAPL trade and write: trend, entry, exit, result.
Log the AAPL trade. Close your paper trade and write one thing you noticed.
tasks.why: Consistent logging builds your personal database of what works.

variants: Count your trades. Look back at your notes and count how many paper trades you've made.
Review your log. Read through all your trade notes from the past week.
Check your history. Open your paper trade log and count how many trades you've placed.
tasks.why: Seeing your trade history reminds you how much progress you've made.

variants: Count winners and losers. Write how many of your paper trades made money vs lost.
Find your win rate. Look at your trade log and count profitable vs unprofitable trades.
Track your results. Write: X winning trades and Y losing trades so far.
tasks.why: Knowing your win rate is the first step to improving it.

variants: Spot a pattern. Write one thing your winning trades had in common.
Find what worked. Look at your winning trades and write what they shared.
Look for clues. Write: 'My best trades all had...' and finish the sentence.
tasks.why: Finding patterns in your own trades is how you build a real edge.

variants: Read your rules. Open your notes and read your 3 trading rules out loud.
Rule check. Find your trading rules and read them before doing anything today.
Review the plan. Open your rule sheet and read it start to finish.
tasks.why: Starting each session with your rules keeps you disciplined.

variants: Take a paper trade. Open Webull Paper Trading, check the trend, and buy SPY.
Trade with intention. Go to Paper Trading, confirm the trend, and place your buy.
Make a mindful trade. Read your rules, check SPY's trend, then buy in Paper Trading.
tasks.why: Intentional trades — where you follow steps — build real skill.

variants: Close and log. Sell your trade and write: trend, entry, exit, result.
Exit and debrief. Close the paper trade and write 2 sentences about what happened.
Review trade #X. Close your trade and log entry, exit, and one lesson learned.
tasks.why: Every logged trade teaches you something — even a small loss.

variants: Learn about market open. Search 'why is market open volatile' and read one short article.
Watch a market-open video. Find a 2-min clip explaining why the first 30 min are risky.
Understand market open. Search 'trading the open for beginners' and watch a short clip.
tasks.why: The first 30 minutes of trading are the riskiest — beginners should know why.

variants: Check SPY at open. Look at SPY's 5-min chart and find the first 3 candles of the day.
Read the open candles. Open SPY's 5-min chart and look at what happened at 9:30 AM.
Study the first candles. Find SPY's first 30 minutes on the 5-min chart and look at the volatility.
tasks.why: Seeing the open on a chart makes the lesson real.

variants: Write a rule about timing. Write: 'I will wait until X minutes after open before trading.'
Set a time rule. Decide: will you trade the open, or wait 15-30 minutes? Write it down.
Add a timing rule. Write one rule about what time you will and won't trade.
tasks.why: Knowing when NOT to trade is as valuable as knowing when to trade.

variants: Read your rules. Open your notes and read all your trading rules before anything else.
Rule review. Find your written rules and read them — add your new timing rule.
Check your plan. Open your trade rules and read them, including your timing rule.
tasks.why: Starting with your rules prevents impulsive decisions.

variants: Paper trade after the open. Wait 15 minutes in the paper session, then buy SPY.
Trade with timing. In Paper Trading, only enter after the first 15-min candle closes.
Apply your timing rule. Use Webull Paper Trading and wait for the right time to enter.
tasks.why: Practicing your timing rule in paper trading builds the real habit.

variants: Close and log. Sell your trade and write: entry time, price, result, and did you follow your rules?
Log this trade. Close the paper trade and write whether you followed your timing rule.
Trade debrief. Close your trade and write: did you wait the right amount of time before entering?
tasks.why: Tracking rule compliance teaches you where you break down.

variants: Learn about risk/reward. Search 'risk reward ratio trading beginners' and watch 2 minutes.
Watch an R:R video. Find a short clip explaining what a 1:2 risk reward ratio means.
Understand R:R. Watch a 2-min beginner video on how to plan a trade's risk vs reward.
tasks.why: A good risk/reward ratio means even with 50% wins you can be profitable.

variants: Calculate R:R on a trade. Pick a price, set a stop 10 cents below, target 20 cents above.
Write a trade plan. Choose a stock, define entry, stop loss, and target before placing anything.
Practice R:R. Write: 'Buy at X, stop at Y, target at Z' and check that reward is 2x risk.
tasks.why: Writing the R:R before a trade removes emotion from decision-making.

variants: Take a planned paper trade. Place the trade you just planned in Webull Paper Trading.
Execute the plan. Go to Paper Trading and enter the trade you wrote out.
Trade with a plan. Use your entry, stop, and target from earlier and place the paper trade.
tasks.why: Executing a planned trade feels different from winging it — better.

variants: Close and review yesterday's trade. Sell your paper trade and check: did price hit your target?
Exit and log. Close your open paper trade and write entry, exit, and whether your plan worked.
Debrief the planned trade. Close your trade and write: did the plan play out as expected?
tasks.why: Comparing what you planned vs what happened is how you learn fast.

variants: Check SPY's trend. Open SPY's 5-min chart and confirm today's trend direction.
Read SPY. Pull up SPY in Webull and note: uptrend, downtrend, or sideways.
Daily chart scan. Open SPY and write: trend direction + above or below the MA.
tasks.why: Checking the trend before every session keeps you in sync with the market.

variants: Plan and place a new trade. Write the plan, then execute it in Webull Paper Trading.
Planned trade. Write your entry, stop, target, then go place it in Paper Trading.
Trade with a written plan. Write 3 lines: entry, stop, target — then place the trade.
tasks.why: Back-to-back planned trades build the habit faster.

variants: Read your rules. Open your notes and read your trading rules — all of them.
Rule read. Find your written rules and read them start to finish.
Pre-session review. Read your trading rules out loud before touching the app.
tasks.why: Reading rules before every session reinforces the discipline habit.

variants: Learn about TSLA. Search 'TSLA day trading beginner' and watch a 2-min clip.
Explore a new stock. Find a short video about Tesla stock and how it trades.
Try a different market. Watch a 2-min clip on a volatile stock like TSLA or NVDA.
tasks.why: Volatile stocks are common in day trading — knowing them broadens your toolkit.

variants: Paper trade TSLA. Open Webull Paper Trading and buy 1 share of TSLA with your rules.
Try TSLA in paper trading. Go to Webull Paper Trading, find TSLA, and place a planned trade.
Trade a new stock. Open Paper Trading, write entry/stop/target for TSLA, then execute.
tasks.why: Applying your rules to a new stock tests whether they're solid.

variants: Close TSLA trade. Sell your TSLA paper position and write the result.
Exit and log TSLA. Close the TSLA paper trade and write: plan vs actual result.
Debrief TSLA trade. Close it and write one thing that was different about trading TSLA vs SPY.
tasks.why: Every new stock teaches you something about how the market works differently.

variants: Compare TSLA and SPY. Look at both charts side by side and note one difference.
Chart comparison. Open TSLA and SPY in Webull and compare their 5-min charts.
Spot the difference. Look at TSLA's chart and SPY's chart and write one observation.
tasks.why: Understanding how stocks behave differently prepares you for real conditions.

variants: Update your rules if needed. Read your 3 rules and revise any that didn't hold up.
Rule audit. After trading TSLA, reread your rules and adjust anything that felt wrong.
Refine your plan. Look at your rules and update any based on what you've learned this week.
tasks.why: Rules should evolve as you learn — updating them is a sign of growth.

variants: Watch a trade video. Search 'how to read a 5-min chart day trading' and watch 2 minutes.
Chart reading practice. Find a short video showing a trader reading a 5-min chart.
Learn from a video. Watch a 2-min clip of someone analyzing a chart before a trade.
tasks.why: Watching an expert read a chart speeds up your pattern recognition.

variants: Apply what you watched. Open SPY's 5-min chart and do what the video showed.
Practice chart reading. Open SPY in Webull and read the chart like the video demonstrated.
Do it yourself. After the video, pull up SPY's 5-min chart and narrate what you see.
tasks.why: Immediately applying a lesson makes it stick faster.

variants: Place a planned trade. Write your SPY trade plan and execute it in Paper Trading.
Trade after reading the chart. Analyze SPY's 5-min chart, write a plan, and place it.
Chart-to-trade. Read SPY's chart, write entry/stop/target, then execute in Paper Trading.
tasks.why: Chart-to-trade is the exact workflow you'll use live one day.

variants: Close trade and debrief. Exit your paper trade and write: was the chart read correct?
Log the result. Close your trade and compare what you expected vs what happened.
Debrief the trade. Sell your position and write: did the chart signal play out?
tasks.why: Comparing prediction vs result is how pattern recognition develops.

variants: Check SPY. Open SPY's 5-min chart and write today's trend direction.
Daily read. Pull up SPY, identify the trend, and note the MA position.
SPY scan. Open Webull and do your 3-point check: trend, MA, volume.
tasks.why: Daily scanning keeps your eyes sharp.

variants: Take one more trade. Plan it out, check the trend, then execute in Paper Trading.
End-of-week trade. Write the plan, confirm the trend, then place it in Webull Paper Trading.
Practice trade. Plan entry, stop, target — then go place it in Paper Trading.
tasks.why: Trading volume builds intuition — every trade counts.

variants: Month 1 recap. Write 3 things you know now that you didn't know 30 days ago.
30-day review. List 3 skills you've built: chart reading, following rules, paper trading.
Look back. Write one thing that surprised you and one thing that clicked this month.
tasks.why: A monthly review shows how much you've grown and keeps you motivated.

variants: Count your paper trade results. Write: total trades, wins, losses, and net P&L.
Trade summary. Find all your paper trade notes and count wins vs losses.
30-day P&L. Add up your paper trading results and write the total.
tasks.why: Seeing your 30-day P&L gives you a baseline to beat next month.

variants: Set a goal for month 2. Write one specific thing you want to improve in the next 30 days.
Month 2 plan. Write: 'In the next 30 days I want to...' and finish with one specific goal.
Define your focus. Write one habit or skill you'll focus on for the next 30 days.
tasks.why: A specific goal for the next phase keeps your progress intentional.

variants: Read your rules. Open your trading notes and read your 3 rules before anything else.
Rule review. Find your written rules and read them out loud right now.
Check your plan. Open your rule sheet and read it — including your timing rule.
tasks.why: Starting every session with your rules keeps your decision-making consistent.

variants: Learn about the bull flag. Search 'bull flag pattern beginners' and watch 2 minutes.
Watch a pattern video. Find a short clip explaining what a bull flag setup looks like.
Study bull flags. Watch a 2-min beginner video on the bull flag chart pattern.
tasks.why: The bull flag is one of the cleanest patterns for beginners to start with.

variants: Find a bull flag. Open SPY's 5-min chart and look for a flag-shaped move.
Spot the pattern. Look at SPY's chart today and see if a bull flag formed anywhere.
Identify a flag. Open Webull, pull up SPY, and circle any area that looks like a bull flag.
tasks.why: Spotting patterns on a real chart is the only way to learn to see them.

variants: Check SPY. Open SPY's 5-min chart and run your 3-point scan: trend, MA, volume.
Daily read. Pull up SPY in Webull and check trend direction + MA position.

Debrief breakout. Close the trade and write: did you wait for 2-candle confirmation?
Grade yourself. Close the trade and rate your confirmation and entry timing.
tasks.why: Breakout trades fail most when you enter too early — grade the timing every time.

variants: Learn about scaling out. Search 'partial profits day trading' and watch 2 minutes.
Watch a scale-out video. Find a clip explaining how to sell part of a position at the first target.
Study partial exits. Watch 2 min on taking half profit at target 1 and letting the rest run.
tasks.why: Partial exits let you lock in profit while still participating in the full move.

variants: Plan a scale-out trade. Write: entry, stop, target 1 (sell half), target 2 (sell rest).
Two-target plan. Write your trade plan with two exits: T1 and T2.
Practice the plan. Write out a two-target exit plan before placing today's paper trade.
tasks.why: Writing two targets forces you to think in terms of reward management.

variants: Execute the scale-out. Place the trade and sell half at T1 when price hits it.
Trade with 2 targets. Place the paper trade and take partial profit at the first target.
Scale out on the trade. Execute in Paper Trading and sell part of the position at T1.
tasks.why: Practicing partial exits on paper makes them feel natural before going live.

variants: Close the remainder and log. Exit the rest of the trade and write the full result.
Finish the scale-out. Close the remaining position and write: did the two-target plan work?
Full debrief. Exit and write: T1 hit? T2 hit? Total result? Would you do it again?
tasks.why: A complete debrief on a two-target trade teaches you whether the approach works for you.

variants: Check SPY. Full 5-point scan.
Daily read. SPY scan — trend, MA, VWAP, volume, structure.
SPY check. 60-second full scan.
tasks.why: Scan first, always.

variants: Take another scale-out trade. Repeat the two-target approach on a new setup.
Practice two-target exits again. Find a new setup and plan T1 + T2.
Two-target repeat. Write entry/stop/T1/T2, then execute in Paper Trading.
tasks.why: Two consecutive two-target trades builds the habit.

variants: Read your rules. Open your notes and read the full rule sheet.
Pre-session review. Read your rules including the two-target approach.
Rule check. Open your rule sheet and read it start to finish.
tasks.why: Your plan is your protection — read it before every session.

variants: Learn about losing streaks. Search 'how to handle losing streak trading' and watch 2 min.
Watch a drawdown video. Find a clip on what to do when you have 3 losses in a row.
Study losing streaks. Watch 2 minutes on how to stay disciplined when you're down.
tasks.why: Losing streaks happen to every trader — knowing how to handle them prevents blowups.

variants: Write a losing streak rule. Write: 'If I lose 3 trades in a row, I will stop for the day.'
Set your circuit breaker. Write the number of losses that will make you stop trading for the day.
Add a daily loss limit. Write: 'My max losses per day is X. If I hit it, I stop.'
tasks.why: A circuit breaker rule prevents a bad day from becoming a catastrophic day.

variants: Check SPY. Full 5-point scan.
Daily read. Open SPY and run your pre-trade check.
SPY scan. Trend + MA + VWAP + volume + structure.
tasks.why: Scan every day — no exceptions.

variants: Learn about position sizing. Search 'position sizing day trading beginners' and watch 2 min.
Watch a size video. Find a clip explaining how many shares to buy relative to your risk.
Study sizing. Watch 2 minutes on how to size a trade based on your stop loss distance.
tasks.why: Sizing correctly is what keeps one bad trade from wiping out a week of gains.

variants: Calculate a position size. Write: if your max risk is $50 and the stop is 10 cents away, how many shares?
Practice sizing. Write: 'Risk $X, stop Y cents away = Z shares' for today's trade.
Size a trade. Before placing your next paper trade, calculate the right number of shares.
tasks.why: Doing the size math before every trade becomes automatic over time.

variants: Trade with correct sizing. Calculate shares, write the plan, execute in Paper Trading.
Sized paper trade. Do the position size math first, then place the trade.
Execute with sizing. Calculate shares based on risk, then execute in Webull Paper Trading.
tasks.why: Trading with correct sizing — even on paper — builds the real habit.

variants: Monitor the trade. Check your P&L in Webull Paper Trading.
Watch the position. Open Paper Trading and see where the sized trade stands.
Track the trade. Check your paper trade and note the current profit or loss.
tasks.why: Watching your P&L in a sized trade teaches you how money moves.

variants: Close and log. Exit and write: entry, size, exit, and total gain or loss in dollars.
Log with dollar amounts. Close the trade and write the result in dollars, not just points.
Debrief. Exit and write: entry, shares, exit, total P&L in dollars.
tasks.why: Logging dollar amounts makes the results feel real.

variants: Read your rules. Full rule sheet — including daily loss limit and sizing rule.
Pre-session review. Read all rules including circuit breaker and position size.
Rule check. Open your notes and read every rule out loud.
tasks.why: Your rules cover every scenario — read all of them.

variants: Watch a trader video. Search 'real day trader paper trading routine' and watch 2 minutes.
See a routine. Find a clip showing a day trader going through their morning routine.
Study a process. Watch 2 min of someone doing a pre-market scan and trade plan.
tasks.why: Watching a real trader's process shows you what yours should look like.

variants: Map your own routine. Write out your step-by-step process: scan, plan, entry, monitor, exit, log.
Define your routine. Write the 5 steps you take from opening Webull to closing a trade.
Document your process. Write your full trading routine from scan to log in 5 bullet points.
tasks.why: Writing down your process reveals gaps and makes it repeatable.

variants: Check SPY. Full 5-point scan as step 1 of your written routine.
Follow your routine. Open SPY and do your scan as the first step of your written process.
Routine start. Step 1 of your routine: scan SPY — trend, MA, VWAP, volume, structure.
tasks.why: Your written routine only works if you follow it every day.

variants: Execute your full routine. Go through every step: scan, plan, entry, monitor, exit, log.
Follow the process. Execute a paper trade by following your written routine exactly.
Test your routine. Go through every step of your documented process on today's trade.
tasks.why: Running your full routine on paper builds the habit that will carry you live.

variants: Debrief the routine. Write: which steps did you follow, and which did you skip?
Routine review. After the trade, check each step off your list and note any gaps.
Grade the process. Write: did you follow every step of your routine today? What was missing?
tasks.why: Grading the process — not the trade — is how professionals measure improvement.

variants: Check SPY. Scan — then check your watchlist.
Daily scan. Open SPY, run the 5-point check, then look at 2 other stocks.
SPY + watchlist. Scan SPY first, then check AAPL and TSLA briefly.
tasks.why: Expanding your scan to 3 stocks gives you more trade opportunities.

variants: Pick the best setup. Look at 3 stocks and choose the one with the cleanest setup.
Compare setups. Open SPY, AAPL, and TSLA — write which one has the best setup today.
Select one trade. Scan 3 charts and pick only the one with the clearest pattern.
tasks.why: Choosing the best setup from multiple options is a key real-trading skill.

variants: Trade the best setup. Execute the paper trade on the stock with the cleanest setup.
Act on your pick. Place the paper trade on the stock you ranked #1.
Trade your selection. Execute on the stock you chose from your 3-stock scan.
tasks.why: Having multiple options and choosing the best one improves trade quality.

variants: Close and log the comparison trade. Exit and write: was the best-setup stock actually the best outcome?
Debrief the selection. Close the trade and write: did the highest-ranked setup perform the best?
Log the multi-stock pick. Exit and write: entry, exit, result, and whether your stock selection was right.
tasks.why: Evaluating your stock selection teaches you what 'best setup' really looks like.

variants: Daily scan. SPY + 2 others. Run the 5-point check on all three.
Check 3 stocks. Scan SPY, AAPL, TSLA — trend direction on each.
Watchlist scan. Open your 3 stocks and note which looks strongest today.
tasks.why: A 3-stock scan daily builds your market awareness.

variants: Take a trade on the best setup. Select from your scan and execute.
Multi-stock trade. Pick the best-looking chart from your scan and place the paper trade.
Trade the top pick. From your 3-stock scan, pick the cleanest chart and execute.
tasks.why: Repeating the selection process builds a reliable decision-making habit.

variants: Read your rules. Open your full rule sheet and read everything.
Pre-session rules. Read your complete trading plan before touching Webull.
Rule review. Read every rule out loud including circuit breaker and sizing.
tasks.why: Your rules are your edge — read them before every session.

variants: Learn about trading psychology. Search 'trading psychology beginners' and watch 2 minutes.
Watch a mindset video. Find a 2-min clip on how emotions affect trading decisions.
Study psychology. Watch 2 minutes on fear and greed in trading.
tasks.why: Trading psychology determines whether your skills show up when it matters.

variants: Note 1 mindset win. Write one thing you did right mentally in your last 5 trades.
Spot your discipline. Look at your trade log and find one example of following your rules under pressure.
Write a mindset moment. Find one trade in your log where you stayed disciplined and write what you did.
tasks.why: Acknowledging mental wins reinforces disciplined behavior.

variants: Daily scan. SPY + 2 others — full 5-point check on SPY.
Check 3 stocks. Open SPY, AAPL, and one other — note the trend on each.
Watchlist read. Scan your 3 stocks and pick the best setup.
tasks.why: Pre-trade scanning is non-negotiable at this stage.

variants: Trade without emotion. Before placing the trade, write: 'I will not move my stop and I will exit at my target.'
Commit to the plan. Write your full plan and add: 'I will follow this exactly.'
Pre-trade pledge. Write your plan, then write: 'I commit to following every rule on this trade.'
tasks.why: Writing a commitment before the trade reduces impulsive decisions during it.

variants: Execute the committed trade. Place the trade and follow your plan exactly.
Trade the commitment. Execute the paper trade and do not deviate from the written plan.
Follow through. Place the trade you planned and stick to every written rule.
tasks.why: Following a written commitment builds the discipline muscle.

variants: Close and grade. Exit and write: did you follow the plan exactly? Score 1-10.
Commitment debrief. Close the trade and rate how well you followed your pre-trade commitment.
Discipline grade. Exit the trade and give yourself an honest score on rule compliance.
tasks.why: Grading your discipline score over time shows whether you're improving as a trader — not just a chart reader.

variants: Daily scan. SPY + watchlist.
Check 3 stocks. Run your scan and note the best setup.
Scan first. Open Webull and check SPY, AAPL, and one other before anything.
tasks.why: Scanning before every trade is the most important single habit.

variants: Take a clean trade. Execute the best setup you find today using your full process.
Full-process trade. Scan, select, plan, execute, and log using your documented routine.
Run the routine. Go through every step of your written process on today's trade.
tasks.why: Every full-process rep builds toward a real trading routine.

variants: Day 75 review. Write 3 things you do consistently now that show real progress.
Progress check. Write 3 habits from your routine that are now automatic.
Look back at day 45. Write how your process has improved since the halfway point.
tasks.why: Recognizing consistent habits builds confidence in the process.

variants: Run your stats. Count your last 20 trades and write win rate, avg win, avg loss.
Stats check. Calculate your win rate and P&L for the past 15 days of trading.
Numbers review. Count wins and losses from days 61-75 and write the win rate.
tasks.why: Stats every 15 days tell you whether you're on a real improving trend.

variants: Define final 15-day goal. Write one specific metric to hit in days 76-90.
Set the finish line. Write: 'In days 76-90, I will achieve...' and name a measurable goal.
Final sprint goal. Write one thing — win rate, P&L, process score — you'll hit in the last 15 days.
tasks.why: A specific final goal makes the last stretch purposeful.

variants: Read your rules. Full rule sheet — out loud.
Pre-session review. Open your rule sheet and read every rule.
Rule check. Read your trading plan from start to finish before doing anything.
tasks.why: Last 15 days — hold the standard on your rules.

variants: Polish the entry. Find a setup, wait for the perfect candle, then enter — not before.
Entry precision drill. Identify a setup and wait for the exact candle your rules call for.
Practice patience. Find a setup on SPY and wait for the confirmation before buying.
tasks.why: Entry precision is the last thing beginners master — drill it now.

variants: Close and grade the entry. Did you wait for the right candle? Grade 1-10.
Entry debrief. Close the trade and write: was the entry on the right candle or did you jump in early?
Log the entry grade. Exit and write your entry timing score and what you'd change.
tasks.why: Grading entries specifically builds pinpoint precision.

variants: Daily scan. SPY + 2 others — pick the best setup.
Check 3 stocks. Run your scan and identify the cleanest chart.
Scan first. Open Webull and check your 3 watchlist stocks.
tasks.why: Stock selection quality determines trade quality.

variants: Polish the exit. Trade today with a focus on hitting your exact target — no early sells.
Exit precision drill. Set your target and do not close before price hits it.
Practice exit discipline. Place the trade and commit: don't close early, don't move the target.
tasks.why: Exiting too early is the most common mistake of profitable beginners — fix it now.

variants: Close at target and log. Did you hold until the target? Grade 1-10.
Exit debrief. Close the trade at the target and write: did you hold the full trade?
Log the exit grade. Write your exit discipline score and what pulled you toward closing early.
tasks.why: Grading exit discipline makes you aware of the impulse to exit early.

variants: Read your rules. Full rule sheet before anything.
Pre-session review. Read every rule — entry, exit, stop, sizing, circuit breaker.
Rule check. Open your notes and read your complete trading plan.
tasks.why: Rules before session — no exceptions in the final stretch.

variants: Polish stop discipline. Trade today and do NOT move the stop — no exceptions.
Stop discipline drill. Set your stop before entering and commit to not touching it.
Practice the hard rule. Enter the trade, set the stop, and leave it alone no matter what.
tasks.why: Moving your stop is the #1 way beginners turn small losses into big ones.

variants: Log stop discipline. Close the trade and write: did you move the stop? Grade 1-10.
Stop debrief. Exit and write: was the stop exactly where you set it, or did you move it?
Grade stop compliance. Write your stop discipline score and what tempted you to move it.
tasks.why: Naming the temptation to move your stop makes it easier to resist next time.

variants: Daily scan. SPY + 2 others.
Check 3 stocks. Run your scan and pick the best chart.
Scan first. Check SPY, AAPL, and one more — pick the top setup.
tasks.why: Stock selection from a scan beats chasing the first thing you see.

variants: Simulate a losing trade. Take a paper trade you expect to stop out and let it stop out.
Practice taking the loss. Place a trade with a tight stop and let price stop you out if it does.
Accept the stop. Place a trade, set the stop, and accept whatever outcome the market gives.
tasks.why: Willingly accepting a stop loss is a skill — most traders avoid it and overstay losses.

variants: Log the stopped trade. Write: did you accept the loss cleanly, or did you freeze?
Debrief taking the loss. Close the trade and write how it felt to let the stop hit.
Grade loss acceptance. Write: 'When the stop hit, I...' and finish the sentence honestly.
tasks.why: Accepting losses cleanly is the mark of a mature trader.

variants: Watch a real trader. Search 'day trader paper trading live' and watch 2 minutes.
Find a trading video. Watch 2 min of someone placing a paper trade in real time.
See it in action. Find a 2-min clip of a trader executing a full trade from entry to exit.
tasks.why: Watching a real trader execute confirms that your process matches real practice.

variants: Compare their process to yours. Write one thing they did that you also do.
Find the match. Watch the video and write one step in their process that matches yours.
Compare notes. After the video, write: what did they do that you do? What was different?
tasks.why: Comparing your process to a real trader's reveals gaps.

variants: Apply one thing from the video. Execute a paper trade using one new thing you saw.
Borrow a technique. Take one thing from the video and apply it on today's trade.
Test it. Find something in the video you don't do yet and try it on today's paper trade.
tasks.why: Immediately applying what you learn makes it stick.

variants: Read your rules. Open your complete rule sheet and read every word.
Pre-session review. Full rule sheet — out loud if possible.
Rule check. Read your trading plan including the new technique from yesterday.
tasks.why: Rules + new techniques — read them together.

variants: Run a full routine trade. From scan to log, follow every step of your written process.
Full process rep. Scan, pick, plan, execute, monitor, exit, log — no shortcuts.
Complete routine. Go through every step of your documented trading routine today.
tasks.why: The last 10 days are about making the process automatic.

variants: Grade the full process. Score each step 1-5: scan, selection, plan, entry, exit, log.
Process debrief. Rate each step of your routine and find the weakest one.
Full routine grade. Write a score for every step and identify what needs one more push.
tasks.why: Grading every step shows you exactly where to focus in the final 9 days.

variants: Daily scan. SPY + 2 others — full 5-point check on each.
Check 3 stocks. Open your watchlist and identify the best setup.
Scan first. SPY + 2 others before touching anything else.
tasks.why: Scanning before every session is non-negotiable.

variants: Focus on your weakest step. Take a trade and give extra attention to the step you scored lowest.
Drill the weak point. Do your routine with full focus on the step you rated lowest.
Improve one step. Execute today's trade with deliberate focus on your lowest-scored routine step.
tasks.why: Drilling your weakest step accelerates your overall process quality.

variants: Re-grade the weak step. Close the trade and score the step you focused on — did it improve?
Debrief the improvement. Write: did the focused step improve today vs yesterday's score?
Log the progress. Close the trade and compare today's score on the weak step to yesterday.
tasks.why: Measuring improvement on a specific step proves you're getting better.

variants: Read your rules. Full rule sheet.
Pre-session rules. Read your complete plan before anything.
Rule check. Open your notes and read every rule you've written.
tasks.why: Consistent rule-reading is what separates disciplined traders from gamblers.

variants: Simulate a day with 2 losses. Take 2 paper trades knowing you might lose both.
Two-loss simulation. Place 2 trades back to back and see how you handle 2 stops in a row.
Practice the bad day. Place 2 trades and if both stop out, do you stop? Follow your circuit breaker.
tasks.why: Simulating a bad day on paper prepares you emotionally for the real thing.

variants: Log both trades. Write the result of each and whether you activated your circuit breaker.
Debrief the 2-loss day. Write: how did it feel? Did you want to take a third trade to 'make it back'?
Write the emotional note. After 2 losses, write: what was your instinct, and did you follow your rules?
tasks.why: Writing about the impulse to overtrade after losses is how you recognize and prevent it.

variants: Daily scan. SPY + 2 others.
Check 3 stocks. Scan and select.
Scan first. Open Webull and check your 3 stocks.
tasks.why: Scan every session — especially after a tough day.

variants: Take one clean trade. Full process, no shortcuts, no emotion.
Clean execution. Run your full routine on one high-quality setup.
One perfect trade. Find the best setup, follow every step, execute cleanly.
tasks.why: Coming back focused after a simulated rough day is real resilience training.

variants: Grade the clean trade. Score each step and compare to your recent average.
Debrief the bounce-back trade. Write: did yesterday's simulation make you more careful or more hesitant?
Log the recovery trade. Write entry, exit, result, and your mental state during it.
tasks.why: Logging how you trade after a tough day teaches you your real psychology.

variants: Read your rules. Full rule sheet — the whole thing.
Pre-session review. Read your complete trading plan out loud.
Rule check. Open your notes and read all rules including your most recent additions.
tasks.why: You've written good rules — reading them is respecting your own work.

variants: Write a 'ready to go live' checklist. List 5 things you need to be true before trading real money.
Define readiness. Write 5 criteria for when you'll be ready to trade live.
Create your go-live checklist. Write 5 specific conditions you must meet before using real money.
tasks.why: A clear go-live checklist prevents rushing into real money before you're ready.

variants: Grade yourself on the checklist. Score 1-10 on each of your 5 readiness criteria.
Check yourself. Go through your go-live list and honestly score where you are.
Self-assess. Rate your current readiness on each of your 5 criteria.
tasks.why: Honest self-assessment shows you exactly what to work on in the final 5 days.

variants: Daily scan. Full 5-point check on SPY + 2 others.
Check 3 stocks. Scan your watchlist and select the best setup.
Scan first. SPY + 2 others before anything.
tasks.why: Scan habits don't fade in the final week — they compound.

variants: Work on your lowest readiness score. Take a trade that specifically targets the weakness.
Drill your gap. Find the lowest checklist score and design today's trade around improving it.
Target the weak criterion. Execute a trade with explicit focus on your lowest readiness score.
tasks.why: The final days are about closing gaps — not just piling on volume.

variants: Re-score that criterion. After the trade, re-rate the readiness item you focused on.
Debrief the targeted practice. Write: did focused practice on that criterion move the needle?
Log the improvement. Close the trade and compare your before/after score on the target criterion.
tasks.why: Measuring targeted improvement proves you're closing the gap.

variants: Read your rules. Full rule sheet — one more time.
Pre-session rules. Read your complete trading plan.
Rule check. Open your notes and read every rule you've ever written.
tasks.why: Consistent rule-reading through day 90 cements the habit.

variants: Run a full sim day. Treat today like a real trading day — scan, select, plan, trade, log.
Full simulation. Execute your complete routine as if this were your first real-money day.
Real-day dress rehearsal. Follow your full process as if real money is on the line today.
tasks.why: A dress rehearsal is the best preparation for going live.

variants: Debrief the sim day. Write: if this were real money, would you be comfortable with how you traded?
Sim day review. Close the trade and write: what went well, and what would scare you with real money?
Full debrief. Write: on a scale of 1-10, how ready do you feel to trade real money after today?
tasks.why: Honest readiness self-assessment is the most important thing you can do in these final days.

variants: Daily scan. SPY + 2 others — full 5-point check.
Check 3 stocks. Scan and select the cleanest setup.
Scan first. Open Webull and run your pre-trade check.
tasks.why: Scan every session — this habit carries directly into live trading.

variants: Score your 90-day readiness. Go through your go-live checklist and re-rate every criterion.
Updated readiness check. Score all 5 readiness criteria honestly with 3 days to go.
Checklist review. Open your go-live checklist and give an honest current score to each item.
tasks.why: An honest final readiness check tells you what the last 2 days should focus on.

variants: Fill the biggest gap. Take a trade targeting your lowest readiness score one more time.
Last targeted drill. Find your weakest criterion and execute a trade focused on it.
Close the last gap. Drill the lowest-scored readiness item with one more focused practice trade.
tasks.why: Closing the last gap before day 90 is the most valuable use of time now.

variants: Read your rules one more time. Open your full rule sheet and read every word.
Pre-final review. Read your complete trading plan from start to finish.
Rule check. Open your notes and read everything — one day before the end.
tasks.why: Reading your rules on day 89 is a tribute to how far you've come.

variants: Take one final high-quality trade. Best setup, full process, no shortcuts.
Last practice trade. Find the best chart on your watchlist and execute perfectly.
Clean execution. Scan, select, plan, execute, monitor, exit, log — all steps, no exceptions.
tasks.why: A clean final practice trade is the best thing you can do before day 90.

variants: Write your final trade debrief. Score every step and write your overall readiness number.
Grade the final practice. Score each routine step and write: am I ready?
Last debrief. Close the trade, grade every step, and write a readiness score out of 10.
tasks.why: Your final practice debrief is the data you'll carry into live trading.

variants: 90-day review. Write 5 things you can do now that you couldn't do on day 1.
Final recap. List your 5 biggest skills: scan, patterns, rules, process, discipline.
Look back at day 1. Write how different your trading looks compared to 90 days ago.
tasks.why: Reflecting on the full arc shows you how much you've actually built.

variants: Final stats. Write total trades, win rate, and net P&L for the full 90 days.
Complete P&L. Look at your paper trading account and write the final balance.
90-day numbers. Count all trades and write: total, wins, losses, win rate, P&L.
tasks.why: Your final stats are your baseline — the number to beat when you go live.

variants: Write your go-live plan. Using your checklist, decide: am I ready, and what's the first step?
Define next steps. Write: will you trade live? If yes, what's your starting account size and first rule?
Write your live plan. Set the date, the account size, and the max daily loss for your first real trading day.
tasks.why: Day 90 is the beginning, not the end — your live plan is the most important thing you write today.
